title: Nasya: nose drops for head and senses
excerpt: Nasya, the Ayurvedic practice of putting a few drops of medicated oil into each nostril, takes under two minutes a day and quietly supports your head, sinuses, voice, and mental clarity.
body_html:
the problem you already know

Most of us wake up with a faintly blocked nose, tight shoulders around the neck, or a dull heaviness behind the eyes. We reach for coffee, hot water, or a shower, hoping things clear up. Ayurveda noticed this pattern thousands of years ago and offered a small, precise remedy: nasya, the practice of introducing medicated oil or ghee into the nostrils as part of morning routine.

The name comes from the Sanskrit nasa, meaning nose. In classical Ayurveda, the nose is called shirodvara, the doorway to the head. Whatever enters through it acts directly on the structures above: the sinuses, the sense organs, the channels (srotas) leading to the brain. This is not metaphor; it is the anatomical reasoning you find laid out in the Ashtanga Hridaya, where nasya is listed as one of the panchakarma-adjacent therapies specifically because of its reach (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 20).

the nose is the gateway to the head

what the granthas actually say

Caraka Samhita describes nasya in detail within its discussion of dinacharya, the ideal daily routine. The text recommends daily nasal application of oil as a measure for protecting the sense organs and maintaining the health of the head, ears, eyes, nose, and throat (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 5). The phrase used is nasa hi shiraso dwaram, which translates simply as 'the nose is the gateway to the head'. This single line captures the entire rationale of the practice.

Sushruta Samhita, approaching the subject from a surgical and therapeutic angle, classifies nasya into several types based on the substance used and the intention: pratimarsha nasya (the gentle daily type using a small amount of oil), marsha nasya (a larger therapeutic dose), and avapidana nasya (using expressed plant juices for specific conditions) (Sushruta Samhita, Chikitsasthana). For most people going about ordinary life, pratimarsha nasya with anu taila or plain sesame oil is all that is recommended.

Ashtanga Hridaya, a later synthesis that distilled the two main traditions, is especially clear about the benefits claimed: clear voice, strong jaw, firm teeth and gums, bright eyes, and freedom from disorders of the head and neck (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 20). It lists nasya among the daily practices because, in the Ayurvedic view, the head accumulates vata and kapha through the night, and a small amount of unctuous substance in the morning gently pacifies both.

how to do it safely at home

The version meant for daily use is pratimarsha nasya, and it requires almost nothing. You need two or three drops of warm anu taila (a classical medicated sesame oil available at Ayurvedic pharmacies) or plain cold-pressed sesame oil per nostril. The right time is after your morning hygiene and before eating, when the stomach is empty.

Tilt your head back slightly, either lying down or sitting upright. Place two drops in one nostril, close the other with a finger, and inhale gently. Repeat on the other side. Then sniff lightly two or three times so the oil travels upward. You can massage the sides of the nose and the base of the skull gently afterward. The whole thing takes ninety seconds.

A few things to avoid: do not do nasya on the same day as shirobhyanga (head oil massage) if you have congestion, avoid it if you have a cold or fever, skip it on days of fasting, and avoid it immediately after a bath where you have washed your hair with cold water. The classical texts are careful to list contraindications because the nose is a sensitive channel: during pregnancy, in very young children, and in people with active respiratory infections, stronger forms of nasya should be administered only under supervision (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 20).

With the gentle daily version, side effects are rare. You may feel a slight warmth in the sinuses, or notice some mucus loosening the first few mornings. That is normal and settles within a week.

when to see a doctor or vaidya

Home nasya with a plain or lightly medicated oil is gentle, but it is not a substitute for medical care. See a qualified physician or vaidya if you have persistent nasal blockage lasting more than two to three weeks, blood in nasal discharge, severe headaches, loss of smell, swelling around the eyes or forehead, or any history of nasal polyps, deviated septum, or recent head injury. These may indicate conditions that need examination and treatment, not oil drops.

Therapeutic nasya with stronger preparations, longer retention times, or herbal powders should only be done under the guidance of a trained Ayurvedic practitioner. The information here is general in nature and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for professional medical advice. If anything feels wrong during or after the practice, stop and consult a doctor.

Try This Today

  • Warm the oil first: Place your small bottle of sesame or anu taila in a cup of warm water for two minutes before use. Cold oil in the nose is jarring; warm oil is absorbed more smoothly and feels comfortable.
  • Do it before breakfast: The classical recommendation is an empty stomach. Nasya on a full stomach can cause nausea. Keep your bottle next to your toothbrush so the habit links naturally to your morning routine.
  • Gentle self-massage after: After the drops, use your fingertips to massage gently up the sides of the nose, across the cheekbones, and at the base of the skull for thirty seconds. This helps the oil travel and relieves morning tension in the head and neck.
  • Start with just one drop per side: If you are new to nasya or find two drops uncomfortable, begin with one drop per nostril for the first week. The goal is a mild, pleasant sensation, not discomfort.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: Nasya, the Ayurvedic practice of putting a few drops of medicated oil into each nostril, takes under two minutes a day and quietly supports your head, sinuses, voice, and mental clarity.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Two to three drops of warm sesame or anu taila oil in each nostril every morning is the classical daily nasya practice.
The nose is called the gateway to the head in Ayurvedic texts, making nasya effective for sinuses, voice, eyes, and mental clarity.
Skip nasya during colds, fever, pregnancy, and in young children without professional guidance.

glossary_terms.definition: Nasal administration of oil, ghee, or herbal preparations; one of the classical Ayurvedic daily practices and a component of panchakarma.
Literally 'gateway to the head'; the Ayurvedic term for the nose, reflecting its role as the channel through which substances reach the head.
The gentle daily nasya using two to four drops of oil per nostril, safe for everyday home practice.
A classical medicated sesame oil formulated specifically for nasya, combining sesame oil with a range of aromatic and clarifying herbs.
The Ayurvedic concept of a daily routine aligned with natural rhythms; nasya is one of its recommended morning practices.
Channels or pathways through which substances, energy, and information move in the body; the nasal channel connects to the channels of the head.

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Nasya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Nasya is defined as: Nasal administration of oil, ghee, or herbal preparations; one of the classical Ayurvedic daily practices and a component of panchakarma.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm the oil first. Place your small bottle of sesame or anu taila in a cup of warm water for two minutes before use. Cold oil in the nose is jarring; warm oil is absorbed more smoothly and feels comfortable.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to do it before breakfast. The classical recommendation is an empty stomach. Nasya on a full stomach can cause nausea. Keep your bottle next to your toothbrush so the habit links naturally to your morning routine.
